tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
  • ADD: 2 occurrences<>
  • INC: 4 occurrences<>
  • LEA: 31 occurrences<>
  • SETAE: 2 occurrences<>
  • VMOVHPD: 2 occurrences<>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround:
Use vector aligned instructions:
  1. The GNU Fortran compiler does not support allocation alignment and does not feature directives to benefit from aligned data.
  2. Use another compiler or locally use C code (for instance via libraries)
<>

The binary loop is loading 3286 bytes (410 double precision FP elements). The binary loop is storing 534 bytes (66 double precision FP elements).

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 160.00 cycles. At this rate:
  • 21% of peak load performance is reached (20.54 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 5% of peak store performance is reached (3.34 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 160.00 to 134.33 cycles (1.19x speedup).

header:
Warnings:
The number of fused uops of the instruction [CLTQ] is unknown
5% of peak computational performance is used (1.31 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
• Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 160.00 to 66.00 cycles (2.42x speedup).

gain.workaround:
• Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly: Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)<>
    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA): do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
18% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
  • 25% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 1% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 17% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 16% of SSE/AVX multiply instructions are used in vector version.
  • 16% of SSE/AVX fused multiply-add instructions are used in vector version.
  • 21% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is not vectorized.
Only 18% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 160.00 to 37.46 cycles (4.27x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

potential.workaround:
Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
Estimated speedup by perfect pairing: 1.01x.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).
potential.title: FMA
potential.txt:
Detected 18 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.

header:
The loop is defined in /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:684-705. Analyzed code is defined in /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:684-692,695-700,705. Warnings:
  • Non-innermost loop: analyzing only self part (ignoring child loops).
  • Ignoring paths for analysis
  • Too many paths. If you really need to analyze all of the 680 paths individually, rerun with max-paths=680
  • RecMII not computed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
  • Streams not analyzed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
Try to simplify control and/or increase the maximum number of paths per function/loop through the 'max-paths-nb' option. This loop has 680 execution paths. The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck. Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
  • hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
  • turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX
Ex: if (x<0) x=0 => x = max(0,x) (Fortran instrinsic procedure)
